title: Tandem Exclusion Process
authors: Ngo Phuoc Nguyen Ngoc, Lam Thi Nhung, Huynh Anh Thi
abstract: We introduce the \emph{tandem exclusion process} (TEP), a one-dimensional stochastic lattice model motivated by tandem running in ants. Particles evolve through two cooperative local transitions, $110\to101$ at rate $α$ (leader advancement) and $101\to011$ at rate $β$ (follower recovery). We prove that the stationary measure on the dynamically active sector is the Gibbs measure $π\propto q^{H(η)}$, where $q=β/α$ and $H(η)$ counts neighboring occupied pairs, and derive exact closed-form expressions for the stationary current and spatial correlations using transfer-matrix methods. The current is asymmetric under particle--hole exchange $ρ\mapsto1-ρ$, with its maximum occurring at densities strictly larger than $1/2$, in contrast to the symmetric current $ρ(1-ρ)$ of the totally asymmetric simple exclusion process (TASEP). For $q>1$, cooperative dynamics enhances the current above the TASEP value and generates strong spatial clustering; in the limit $q\to\infty$, the current approaches $J\toαρ$, corresponding to nearly unconstrained collective transport. These results suggest that tandem coordination alone can substantially enhance collective transport efficiency at moderate and high densities, even without pheromone-mediated long-range communication.
summary: This paper analyzes the Tandem Exclusion Process (TEP), a lattice model simulating cooperative particle movement. It proves the system reaches a Gibbs stationary state and derives exact formulas for the particle current and spatial correlations. The findings show that tandem coordination can significantly enhance collective transport efficiency, especially at high densities.
main_problem: The paper investigates how local, cooperative interactions generate macroscopic transport phenomena in a non-equilibrium lattice gas model.
main_result: The stationary measure is a Gibbs state, and the resulting particle current is asymmetric, showing enhanced transport efficiency at high densities compared to simpler models like TASEP.
method: The analysis employs transfer-matrix methods and the derivation of the stationary measure via the global balance identity.
model_or_system: The Tandem Exclusion Process (TEP) is a 1D stochastic lattice model simulating particle movement based on two cooperative local transitions (leader advancement and follower recovery).
key_observables: Stationary particle current (J), nearest-neighbor pair correlation ratio (g), and asymptotic correlation length (xi).
important_parameters: The ratio q = beta/alpha, particle density (rho), and the comparison to the TASEP model.
main_assumptions: The analysis is restricted to the dynamically active sector of the lattice configurations, as certain patterns lead to absorbing states.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the dependence of the current on density and the pair correlation ratio g on the parameter q, showing crossovers between clustered and unclustered regimes.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the Gibbs stationary measure, derives closed-form expressions for the current and correlations using transfer-matrix methods, and analyzes the behavior in various parameter regimes (e.g., q->infinity).
why_interesting: The study provides a concrete example of how local, cooperative rules can lead to collective, non-trivial macroscopic transport properties, which is relevant for understanding driven many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Stationary current J(ρ; α, β) for q ∈{0.5, 1, 2} with α = 1. Solid curves: exact formula (10). Filled circles: Monte Carlo simulations (N = 60, 5 × 105 updates).

Figure 1 compares formula (10) with Monte Carlo sim- ulations over the full density range, showing excellent agreement at every value of q, and Figure 2 displays the corresponding phase diagram in the (ρ, q)-plane. The current exhibits three structural features, all originating from the cooperative nature of the dynamics.
FIG. 2. Phase diagram of the stationary current J(ρ, q) with α = 1. Colors encode the current magnitude from low (dark) to high (bright); the dashed line q = 1 marks the Bernoulli locus.

FIG. 3. Spacetime diagrams (N = 80, ρ = 0.5, α = 1). Left: dispersed regime (q = 0.05). Center: Bernoulli regime (q = 1). Right: clustered regime (q = 20), where coherent moving tandem groups appear as broad diagonal bands.

FIG. 4. Pair correlation ratio g = Eπ[η0η1]/ρ2 as a function of q = β/α for several densities. Solid curves: exact formula (16). Filled circles: Monte Carlo simulations (N = 50, 8000 samples). The crossover between clustered (g > 1) and dispersed (g < 1) regimes occurs at q = 1.
